pattern: Because every letter's per-step transformation rule is fixed regardless of how many times it repeats, one transformation step is really a linear map on the 26 letter-counts, so t transformations is that map raised to the t-th power -- computable by fast matrix exponentiation even when t is enormous.
hints: As before, track only how many characters of each of the 26 letters exist rather than the literal string; the tricky part is that t can be up to a billion, far too many to simulate step by step.
One transformation step is a fixed linear function from the 26 old counts to the 26 new counts (letter i's count flows into the next nums[i] letters), so represent that step as a 26x26 matrix.

Raise that matrix to the t-th power using fast exponentiation by squaring (log(t) matrix multiplications, each 26x26), then apply the resulting matrix once to the starting counts and sum the result modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, a uniform shift with one double-length wraparound
statement example, every letter doubles in one step
every shift amount is 1, behaves like the simple version
single letter with a large per-letter shift over a couple of steps
t at its smallest legal value
maximum shift amount on every letter for one step
a single wraparound letter with a shift larger than one
moderately large t exercising repeated squaring, kept small enough to brute-force check

stress.description: long random string with a random per-letter shift table and a huge t, exercising matrix exponentiation
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz') for _ in range(n))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 25) for _ in range(26)]
    t = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    return [s, t, nums]
